correctness.justification: All 11/11 acceptance tests pass. Implementation correctly provides: async context manager yielding pool with submit(), backpressure via asyncio.Queue(maxsize), exactly max_workers concurrent workers, drain-on-exit via queue.join() then sentinel values, error capture and re-raise, ExceptionGroup for multiple errors, and RuntimeError on post-exit submit.

edge_cases.justification: All spec edge cases handled: max_workers=1 serial execution, max_queue=1 extreme backpressure, max_queue=0 and max_workers=0 raise ValueError, zero-task clean exit, error with remaining work still drains, post-exit submit raises RuntimeError. The sentinel-based shutdown (None in queue) is clean and correct.

code_quality.justification: Well-structured with _BoundedPool class separated from the context manager. Good use of asyncio.Queue for backpressure. Type hints present on class and methods. The queue type annotation on line 19 and 63 is very long and hard to read -- would benefit from a TypeAlias. The _close() method uses a simple boolean flag which is clean. Missing return type annotation on bounded_pool function itself.

documentation.justification: Good module-level docstring explaining the overall design. Both bounded_pool() and submit() have docstrings with Args, Yields/Returns, and Raises sections. The _BoundedPool class has a brief docstring. Could be improved with usage examples and a note about the fire-and-forget semantics (results discarded).

test_coverage.justification: 12 tests organized into clear sections: validation (2), happy path (4), backpressure (1), error propagation (3), post-exit (1), kwargs (1). Covers concurrency tracking with max_concurrent assertion, serial execution with max_workers=1, and ExceptionGroup handling. Missing: test for negative max_workers/max_queue values, test that kwargs AND args work together, stress test with many tasks.

robustness.justification: Good error handling: validates max_workers and max_queue at construction, catches BaseException (not just Exception) in workers, properly drains remaining work before re-raising errors, marks pool as closed in finally block. The queue.join() before sentinels ensures all work completes. Minor gap: if queue.put(None) for sentinels blocks because queue is full of real work, this could theoretically deadlock -- but in practice queue.join() ensures all items are consumed first, so this is safe.
